Okay, so we are concluding kind of our three week series on ideas here. Ryan's going to lead most of this one, but to kick it off, I thought we could do something really quick. Two sessions ago, Ryan gave out this big idea template and gave out a couple handouts, and one of those handouts had kind of the, it listed an idea that was like the ordinary idea and then the extraordinary way that someone turned that into a big idea. So you guys remember that handout, right? So it was like, I'll do the one that I'm most familiar with. The ordinary idea was that you can earn big income checks by selling books. And the extraordinary out of that idea was, watch as I show three random strangers on the streets of Baltimore how to instantly generate $127, right? So you had the ordinary and then you had the extraordinary. So I thought today, because we're all reading a promo, okay? Except for Lindsay, she gets passed because she's new. So say hi, Lindsay. She's the only one that gets passed. I thought we could go around really quick. Show me the promo that you read yesterday. I want to know the ordinary idea behind that promo, and I want to know how that person turned it extraordinary if they did. So I'm going to start. The ordinary idea, well, I'll tell you first. The promo that I read yesterday was the Oxford Club Bond promo. If you guys have not read this, you should read this. I believe the promo closes down today or tomorrow. It's been the one at the top of the web batch pretty consistently over the course of the past week. I read it for two reasons. Number one, because it's at the top of the web batch pretty consistently over the past two weeks, and number two, we have a bond service that we have yet to figure out how to sell it. So I wanted to see how they sold it. So the headline is dirty, rotten, no good scumbags, right? So the ordinary out of this is discounted corporate bonds. And the extraordinary is that Wall Street is hiding a secret from you because they trade bonds, they keep it from you, and they're earning huge amounts. But they don't tell you about it because there's no commission in bonds. That's the way that they made it extraordinary. So I'm going to put somebody on the spot. Alex, you're next. Okay, so I did the infinite power one that we talked about on Tuesday. And so the idea is that this small government defense contractor... Is this the ordinary or the extraordinary idea? This is the ordinary idea. Ordinary idea. So the defense contractor is going to get a boost in contracts from the government that's going to have to share price. The extraordinary idea is teasing out that it has infinite power, and therefore it's going to solve all these different military problems and just completely capture the market for it. Is this the ordinary or the extraordinary? The ordinary. Okay. Everybody's going ordinary, extraordinary. You can't see the pattern. Yeah, so... Ordinary. You can turn very little money into a lot of money with three moves and as little as 30 days. And I mean, that idea is pretty simple. And the extraordinary itself is really simple to understand. The only difference is the flash action moves become really important. And as the promos evolved, those flash action moves is where it really gets you going, gets you excited. So the ordinary is you can turn a little bit of money into a lot. The way that it becomes extraordinary is it becomes a 30-day retirement plan with these flash actions. Exactly. Got it. I thought it would illustrate the point, right? So what we're doing is taking ordinary ideas for the most part. This is our job all the time, turning them into extraordinary ideas. So again, the way that we've organized this, the reason that we're spending effectively three weeks on this idea generation stuff, sorry, three sessions on this idea generation stuff, you know, the technique for producing big ideas, right, it's a big idea template, how to reverse engineer other people's big ideas, is because, again, this is the most important part of this entire process. The writing part should be the easiest part. The hardest part, the thing that will make your promotion succeed or fail, is going to be this idea of taking the ordinary and turning it into the extraordinary before you ever put your pen to paper to write a headline or a lead, because if you don't have a good idea, right, it's not going to matter that your writing is brilliant. People don't care about your writing, they care about the idea and the benefit to them. So this is the reason that we're spending so much time on this and the reason why we're going through and reading a single promotion every single day and trying to say, what is the ordinary, what's the extraordinary. So today we're talking about specifically that little book that we handed out at the end of Tuesday's session, the technique for producing big ideas, which is pretty, pretty good. How did I know that this book? So before we just arrive, I want to know why Matt hates the book. Before I give it to Ryan completely on the rest of these notes, I think if you were to update that book for now, take it out of 1940, 1960, whenever it was written, and put it into 2016. And I was telling Lindsay this yesterday too. James Altucher, I think has a more provocative way of saying what this book is about, which is idea sex. It's a little bit more provocative in 2016, which I probably couldn't have said in 1964 or 1960 when the book was written. But Altucher's idea is basically you read a lot, right? So that's kind of like step number one. You have a lot, a lot, a lot of inputs to try to form an idea. So read a lot, read more than anybody else you know. Watch, you know, I think everybody should watch Ted Talk a day too. I think you watch Ted Talk a day. There's multiple, multiple, multiple ideas in all of the Ted Talks because they're so forward looking for the good ones. So you consume as much in the input as you possibly can, right? You read everything, everything like that. Step number two is you kind of, you forget about it, and you let those ideas have sex in your head. So idea number one starts to have sex with idea number two, right? You read a Ted Talk about a new biomarker that could detect cancer early, and it starts to have sex with one of these promos that you've read two weeks ago. So that's the more provocative way to say it. And then step number three is out comes an idea, a baby idea after the sex. That is a combination of two other ideas that have been having sex in your head, right? So that's the more provocative way to say it. I like that way a little bit better. So maybe Matt could write a 13 page book. You boil down the three steps. That says the better technique for producing big ideas. It just says read a lot, let ideas have sex in your head, and watch them have a baby at some point. It's in the public domain. Better technique has a great book, dude. Someone run with that. The technique for producing ideas sex. I didn't go ever as it was. So one piece I thought that was going to go up right around here. One piece I thought that the book did is really useful. Step one is when you gather new materials, new knowledge, is how he breaks apart two different kinds of knowledge that you'll need to write copy. One is specific knowledge versus general knowledge. And I think that's really important. The specific knowledge would be like if you're writing a promotion about corporate bonds, you'd want to get every piece of information you can find about corporate bonds, where they came from, the history of them, the editor, editor's story who's behind the publication. But then there's that piece of general knowledge, which is something that is kind of your permission to be curious about everything. And that's just where you just randomly gather information wherever you can. It can come from anything from books you're reading about the financial markets to watching cable TV shows or watching movies or popular entertainment. David Blaine. Yeah, like the David Blaine example that we talked about a few weeks ago. The Bachelor, I know this. Yeah, the Bachelor. All I have is general knowledge. You want to be building specific knowledge on whatever projects you're working on. But you always got to be building that general knowledge out there for everything. And you don't want to be too much of a nerd, I guess, where you're only talking about cooperating and early studying financial stuff. And you got to have a broad mind. And that's why I'm going to give you guys something special today. You guys up there? Sure. I don't know if I have enough for everybody. All of you in this room have now been promoted to a new job called a sales detective. Keep this in your wallet. Keep it on yourselves at all times. You have to wear it. You have to wear it somewhere on your body at all times. You have to show up like eight of you doobies at the same time. We were going to make you guys flat jackets that said idea detectives, but they got too expensive. So as a sales detective, you now have the right to use all legal means to discover or learn whatever you need to do to find big ideas. This means you can talk to anybody you want in our business or outside of our business. This means you can buy whatever books you want or track down and read whatever books you want. You have permission now to just whatever you see a hint of what could be a big idea to use all legal means to track down every piece of information you can find on it. You want to come down to the studio and we put the single light on you and it's there. There's light detectors somewhere on your face. Exactly. So as Joe mentioned, a big part of the book, a technique producing idea, is that you really don't create big ideas. Really, you're just connecting things. For me, I think it takes the pressure off. You don't have to be some Howard Hughes genius sitting in a dark room. You're really just taking things that are out there in the world and putting them together. It's amazing if you study other cooperators like Gary Halber or Eugene Schwartz. How many of them say basically the same thing? I'll call it here from this handout that I'm giving you guys to read this afterwards. Eugene Schwartz, a brilliant copywriter who wrote a fantastic book called Breakthrough Advertising. This is not actually in the book. Eugene Schwartz said, you don't get an idea or a headline. You dig it out of market research and ring it on the product. You read, you listen, you experiment. The creativity is not in you. It's in your market and your product. All you are doing is joining the two together. We need the market and the product. That's all we need. We're just trying to find some ideas in the marketplace and connect it to our product. I think a key point is that ideas are just looking for new or unusual combinations or connections. The thing that makes IdeaSex even more crazier is the weirder it gets sometimes, the better. I have time to get my camera ready. Don't tell my wife. Part of that though is again, no microgeneral knowledge, a specific knowledge. You need lots of ideas from lots of different inputs. As a copywriter, you should be reading a book a week. It could be the equivalent of a book a week, but I would say that would be a target. Read a book a week. I'll always be reading. Audible is great because if you're in your car or just shopping for groceries, you can still be listening to the book. A book a week. John Caple said, you should collect seven times the amount of information you think you're going to need. Seven times. Gary Bencevenga, another one of the co-operators said, research with a hand truck. You can never have too much information. A lot of people ask me where do I get my ideas. You'll see on that idea sheet that a lot of them are coming from that. I'll just go back over all five ideas every single Monday for the past two years and see what's in there. Real quick, for the people that are somewhat new, what Pete's talking about is as part of Jim's front-end called Strategic Intelligence, he sends out the five links that you need to know each week. What Jim does is he curates content effectively. I don't know how many pieces of news Jim's reading, but he tells you these are the most five important things that I think all readers need to know this week. Then he provides a little summary of them and hyperlinks to that article. That's what Pete's talking about, just reading those alone. I'll second that because I opened a RGS. RGS does the same thing, Rikker's Gold Speculator does the same thing, except it's Byrim, who's curating that content. I feel like in one Rikker's Gold Speculator email with those five links, there were three really good ideas in there, including one that's already had a promo written on it so we couldn't do it, but it was about mining gold from underneath the water. Stansbury wrote a big promo on that same idea a couple of years ago. The company's called Nautilus Minerals, I believe, and the promo is called Gold Stans, about how this huge, huge deposit is untapped and all that's untapped because it's sitting underneath 100 feet of seawater. That same link now showed up in Byrim's Rikker's Gold Speculator link. I'll second that. There's a lot of ideas to be had in there. So that's one way. Yeah, just one other thing on top of that. Five does something like that on Saturday, I think it is. And so the five is a good source. And so does LFT. LFT, Chris does a lot of crazy stuff. I think he's planning on moving down to South America or something. He's going to try and figure, do something different down there. He announced that yesterday, but both of those e-letters are really good because they're forward-looking and they have a smattering of different ideas every single day. Where else, Pete? So, Drudge is on my morning routine. I think that's not a lot of our morning routines, but Drudge is obviously awesome. I've become like a Twitter fiend. To me, that's like Drudge on steroids because you can follow whoever is in our space. So I follow everyone who wouldn't necessarily be an editor with us, but that comes up with ideas that one of our editors could steal and use to make their own. Can I bid on that real quick? So the trick in reading the Drudge is that you don't just read the Drudge. So when you read the Drudge, you're going to read yesterday's news, today's news. The trick when you read the Drudge is to ask yourself a simple question. What comes next if this is true? So if you read this piece of news, right, what comes next from this? That will help you get ideas. So if you read that potentially maybe Russia has been the one who hacked all of these DNC emails and used it to propel Trump into the White House. I would sit there and as a brainstorming extra, I'd say what comes next if that's true? Do we go to war with Russia? What happens if we go to war with Russia? Do we have to build out our nuclear program? Is there some type of war-wide apocalypse because all that happens? So don't just read the news on the Drudge, read the news and say what comes after this? That'll help you get ideas. But if you just read the news and then you're like, oh, my idea is Russia hacked DNC. Well, that's not my idea, that's the news, right? That's the trick. If you read the news, ask yourself what comes next. I would say Drudge is great to study for headlines too. You'll see his headline, it'll be so provocative you click on it and then you'll see the real headline of whatever publication is behind it and you'll be like, that's their headline? Where else Pete? I read Michael Snyder's second on a clubs blog every day. Sometimes he's weird as hell, but a lot of times he does an awesome amount of research. He's a good resource if you're writing a macro promo, by the way, he basically does all your research for you on a particular idea. So real quick, can I stay on macro a second? So what Michael Snyder does at Economic Collapse Blog is you'll see that he'll be like, 72 reasons that the recovery isn't real. And he'll literally list 72 reasons with backup links. So if you're writing a promo on why the recovery isn't real, that's what Pete means by he's done all your research for you. He will literally curate 72 different links. 18 reasons why Detroit will never recover, something like that. You're only going to find bad ideas, not bad ideas, like fierce ideas there. It's called Economic Collapse Blog. You're not going to find how to get rich from pot stocks. But it's a really, really great resource, not only for ideas but for the research, I could say. Other than that, there's just a couple other sites I guess. New Americans kind of like Economic Collapse Blog in that I look at it for those cool kind of proof pieces that maybe could be a new trigger in a promo or something like that. So New American. And then I read the Rational News Digest this call every morning, which is just this libertarian dude who's been putting together news links in an email since like the mid-90s. So he always kind of tells me stories I haven't heard about. And then besides that, I'll do like forced, kind of like forced idea generation where it's like, say, we just, well, Joe, you just met with the defense tech editor. Kind of kicking around potential ideas or what he was thinking about. So that's got me thinking on the types of ideas I'd actually like to have. I don't know what they are and I'm not like waiting around them to hit my inbox or whatever. So I'm just going out on Google and brute forcing it. So I'm like, I'm searching, I'm figuring out every way you could possibly search like cutting edge military technology or something. And then I'm going to like page five all the way through to page five. And then there's some kind of cool thing like 3D printing. I was like really trying to figure out the other day just how could we make a 3D printing idea work for defense tech. So I started searching obviously for like 3D printing military technology or 3D printing military. So I was just stories and I'm like, all right, well, it's being used in the military and there's projects that are funding it. So I'm like, all right, so what's like a cool way to start thinking about a big idea. And then I just widened it and just went to 3D printing itself, figuring that that was like a much general, much more general topic where a lot of people have probably written about it and come up with big ideas, like the turn for it or some way to describe 3D printing. And then I just tried to jam them together. So like if there's a really cool idea that some guy just wrote about 3D printing over here. Now I know that works with the military. It's kind of like the idea sex thing. I just jammed the two together. And so like if you look at like that one on there, I had four ideas. Maybe they all suffer right now. But it was like forever foundries or like mobile battlefields or the endless arsenal. I was trying to come up with like ways to describe what 3D printing would mean to the military. So sticking on that kind of like brute force thing like Pete saying like you have a general idea, but you don't know where that's going to lead you. So to start googling, googling, googling and try to find those links. I'm probably going to mess up this name, but I'm going to try to throw it out there. Is it the Gartman hype? Hype cycle. Gartner. Gartner hype cycle, right? Yeah. Okay, so anybody that's looking at tech ideas, Google that. Gartner. Hype cycle. I'm going to mess that up every time I say it. But basically it's just like, you know, a little curve like this, right? But it takes every technology that's out there, like every talk about technology, and it ranks it of where it believes it's at on the hype cycle. So when we wrote our 3D printing promo, I think it was like right there. And it'll show you technologies that are past the hype cycle too, right? So like maybe this is, I'm just making this up, but maybe this is 3D bio printed. So the sweet spot is like right there, right from a copywriting sign. So take every idea that's in that hype cycle. You guys will be able to find this very easily online. They update it, like how often they update it? Quarterly, semi-annually, annually? I think yearly. Okay, so maybe once a year they're going to update this thing. Take every single technology in there and begin brute force Googling it. And you can apply it to the military too, right? So if you find something on there. So let's say we were just talking, this is top of mind, so again it's probably going to be a bad example. We were talking to the defense tech guy about the idea of cold fusion. A new way to produce, I guess effectively nuclear energy without any of the bad side effects. And it produces more abundant energy than anything else on the planet, but no one's been able to crack this idea of cold fusion. Let's say cold fusion is on that, right? So you can start Googling military uses of cold fusion. Military uses of 3D printing. Military uses of 3D bio printing. You know, maybe that gets you somewhere, maybe it's a dead end. But it's that process of discovery that can help form those new ideas. So if you're looking to write for defense tech alert, just take everything in the hype cycle here. Again, you don't want to write it over here, right? Because that means that it's past the hype. That means people don't care about it anymore. You want to write stuff that's kind of approaching the tipping point. So take everything that's on that upward trajectory and just Google it. And Google it for, you know, if you're writing it for breakthrough technology alert, Google these things and then go back to the editor of breakthrough technology alert and say, hey, I found all this information on wireless energy. What do you guys think of wireless energy? But at least you come prepared in that type of way to form new ideas. It relates to what Addison was talking about with the Bat-Tail ideas, similar curve, remember the allies? The key thing that both Pete and General kind of demonstrated, and it kind of goes back to Joe's point on our last session, is the idea of active versus passive reading. This also applies to anything that you're researching or reading or watching or whatever, where you're not just passively consuming it. You should be training yourself that whenever you find an article somewhere, even if it's just something completely random, a new TV show that you're actively looking at, and be like, how can I make this possibly a copy idea? And that's what the what comes next when you read stuff matters. When you're reading the news, what comes next? What happens if this happens? What happens if that happens? You get to those strange, unique ideas that people have not thought of yet, because you're taking it much further than what most people take the news. And if you're watching TV with somebody and you start doing that, like it drives my wife nuts because she'll be like, why are you just staring into space? Why aren't you watching the show? Or a movie or whatever, it'll pop in my brain and I'll start thinking in the middle of the show. There's probably a direct correlation for how many fights you get into for staring off into space versus how good of a copywriter you are. Or just be in the moment. Just talk about your idea around the time. Yeah, that's a good thing. On the post-A, he said just tie her wife for ideas rather than sex. Yeah, that'll go away. Single million. The string is better. So I'm talking about a couple of specific techniques that I think would be really helpful. One is called the Teenage Mutant Mutant Turtle technique. And the story behind this technique is apparently marked for, you might know, from Agora, Agora Inc. He's been in the business from the beginning. He was sitting in an airplane next to the guy that apparently invented the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles. And he asked the guy, like, where did you come up with that idea? And the guy was like, well, I was, you know, he's like a comic book writer.
